summary: Ronacher argues that OpenID and OAuth share a subtle security weakness rooted in user and developer laziness. Because providers remember recent authentications, sensitive actions on relying sites can be re-approved without a fresh password prompt. He notes that traditional account operations require re-entering a password, but federated login lacks an equivalent local challenge. This makes actions like account deletion or linking a new OpenID identity vulnerable if someone gains access to an already-open browser session. He concludes that current protocols and best-practice guidance do not clearly offer a way to force a re-approval screen at the moment it is needed, leaving users exposed.
key_points: Federated login shifts critical confirmations away from the site, but providers often auto-approve within a session.
Standard account-protection patterns (re-enter password) don’t translate cleanly to OpenID/OAuth.
Attack scenario: an unattended, logged-in browser can be used to perform destructive actions even if the site re-checks OpenID.
OpenID’s ability to attach multiple identities makes the risk worse than OAuth’s per-provider linkage model.
PAPE can limit session lifetime but doesn’t let sites force immediate re-approval when performing sensitive actions.
money_quotes: Enter OpenID's (and OAuth's) security problem: the “remember me for this computer button”.
Notice one thing? All these steps require you to enter a password.
If you are a careful user you will not let the service there remember your password for general authentication, but it will remember you when you come back in the same browser session.
Even if the application does a OpenID auth check to delete the account (which no website I tested does) the OpenID provider will have remembered you.
I might be missing something completely obvious, but there does not seem to be a way to force the OpenID provider to force the user to approve the authentication again (same for OAuth).
Either way I will be very careful to not leave my notebook unattended for a second when a browser is still open.
key_insight: Federated login’s convenience undermines traditional “re-authenticate for sensitive actions” safeguards because providers remember sessions and don’t let sites reliably force a fresh approval.
